note_data.title: Partnership accounts
keyPoints: Manufacturing account is prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
Manufacturing account only includes information about the factory and actual manufacturing process - all other costs go to Income Statement
Cost of production is calculated by adding factory overheads to prime cost
Prime cost (direct cost) is the total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Factory overheads (indirect costs) include costs from running the factory like indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
Transfer price is the production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Factory profit is the difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Work in progress represents inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory, including indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings step by step. Calculate interest on capital, salaries, then remaining profit in profit sharing ratio
Use correct account format with Dr/Cr sides. Appropriation account starts with net profit, current accounts show running balances
Give the reason using 'because' - e.g. 'Goodwill is created because the business has value above its net assets'

modelAnswer.question: Ahmed and Bilal are partners sharing profits 3:2. Net profit for year ended 31 Dec 2023 was $50,000. Ahmed receives salary $8,000, interest on capital: Ahmed $2,000, Bilal $1,500. Prepare appropriation account. (6 marks)
modelAnswer.answer:
APPROPRIATION ACCOUNT for year ended 31 Dec 2023
Net profit $50,000
Less: Salary - Ahmed $(8,000)
Less: Interest on capital - Ahmed $(2,000)
Less: Interest on capital - Bilal $(1,500)
Profit available for distribution $38,500
Share of profit: Ahmed (3/5 × $38,500) $23,100
Share of profit: Bilal (2/5 × $38,500) $15,400
Total distributed $38,500
markBreakdown: Mark 4: Interest on capital correctly deducted

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs like administration, finance, and distribution in the manufacturing account instead of the Income Statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost - prime cost only includes direct costs
Forgetting that cost of production equals prime cost plus factory overheads
examinerTips: Remember the formula: Cost of production = prime cost + factory overheads
Keep manufacturing costs separate from other business costs when preparing accounts
formulas: Interest on capital = Opening capital × Interest rate × Time period
Interest on drawings = Drawings × Interest rate × Average time period
Profit sharing = (Available profit after appropriations) × Partner's ratio
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Partnership accounts structure showing Appropriation Account (distributing net profit) flowing to individual Current Accounts (tracking each partner's running balance) and separate Capital Accounts (recording permanent capital contributions)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a profit and loss appropriation account for a partnership with unequal profit sharing ratios and interest on capital
O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to calculate partners' current account balances after drawings, salary, and profit distribution
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — required preparation of partners' capital and current accounts showing admission of a new partner with goodwill adjustments
O/N 2021 P2 Q4 (7 marks) — tested dissolution of partnership including realization of assets and settlement of partners' accounts

workedExample.question: Amy and Ben are partners sharing profits 3:2. Amy receives salary $6,000 and interest on capital at 5% p.a. Their capitals are Amy $40,000, Ben $30,000. Net profit for the year was $28,000. Prepare the profit and loss appropriation account. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate interest on capital - Amy: $40,000 × 5% = $2,000, Ben: $30,000 × 5% = $1,500
Step 2: Total appropriations before profit share = Salary $6,000 + Interest $3,500 = $9,500
workedExample.answer: Profit and Loss Appropriation Account: Net Profit $28,000. Less: Salary to Amy $6,000, Interest on Capital Amy $2,000, Ben $1,500. Remaining profit $18,500 shared Amy $11,100, Ben $7,400. Total distribution Amy $19,100, Ben $8,900.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: correct interest calculations (2 marks), salary allocation (1 mark), remaining profit calculation (1 mark), correct profit sharing ratio application (1 mark), accurate totals (1 mark)

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to distinguish between capital and current accounts' — always show fixed capital separately from fluctuating current account items like drawings and profit shares
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students incorrectly calculated interest on drawings for the full year' — remember to calculate interest on drawings from the date of withdrawal to year-end only

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates confused goodwill treatment when partners join or leave' — goodwill must be created and then written off, or existing partners' capitals adjusted by their sacrifice ratio
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a T-account layout showing Partners' Current Accounts with debits (drawings, interest on drawings, share of loss) on the left and credits (opening balance, salary, interest on capital, profit share) on the right. Include separate columns for each partner and show the profit-sharing ratio clearly. Also draw the Profit and Loss Appropriation Account showing net profit at the top, then deductions for salaries and interest on capital, followed by the remaining profit distribution according to agreed ratios.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate production costs by adding factory overheads to prime cost (direct materials, labour, and expenses). Only factory-related costs are included - other business costs go to the Income Statement. Transfer pricing involves adding a mark-up to production costs, creating factory profit.
